---
title: "Benchmarking Large Language Models for Structured Metadata Extraction in Development Impact Evaluations: An Autonomous Prompt Optimisation Approach with Human-Curated Quality Gates"
author:
  - name: "Lucas Sempe"
    affiliation: "International Initiative for Impact Evaluation (3ie)"
    email: "l.sempe@3ieimpact.org"
date: "2026-07-10"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
abstract: |
  Evidence synthesis pipelines in international development rely on the manual extraction of
  structured metadata from thousands of impact evaluation reports — a labour-intensive process
  that constrains the speed and scale at which systematic reviews can be produced and updated.
  This paper describes the design, deployment, and evaluation of an AI-enabled data extraction
  system benchmarking twelve large language models (LLMs) across five structured fields drawn
  from 3ie's Development Evidence Portal corpus. Using human-curated ground truth across 100
  impact evaluations, we evaluate model accuracy using field-type-aware quality gates — element-level
  micro-F1 for multi-value list fields, record-level accuracy and Cohen's κ for categorical
  classifications — supplemented by a cross-family LLM concordance judge and a recall floor
  constraint. We further describe a production-deployed autonomous prompt optimisation loop
  (GEPA-lite) that iteratively refines per-model extraction instructions against the quality
  gate until convergence, and a governance architecture that separates autonomous prompt
  improvement from human-approved changes to ground truth and evaluation logic. Results show
  wide variation in baseline performance across models and fields; autonomous optimisation
  substantially narrows accuracy gaps on list fields but reveals systematic limits on
  fine-grained taxonomic classification that point toward ground-truth rather than prompt
  deficiencies. We discuss implications for quality standards, cost-quality tradeoffs, and the
  design of human-on-the-loop governance for AI-assisted evidence synthesis.
keywords:
  - large language models
  - evidence synthesis
  - data extraction
  - prompt optimisation
  - systematic review
  - development effectiveness
  - human-on-the-loop
---

# Introduction

The production of high-quality systematic reviews and evidence maps in international
development is constrained at every stage by the volume and diversity of the literature it
must process. Among the most time-consuming of these stages is structured data extraction:
the laborious task of reading each included study and recording, field by field, the
bibliographic, institutional, and thematic metadata that will later underpin the review's
analysis and dissemination. For a major evidence map covering hundreds or thousands of
studies, this stage alone can consume months of skilled analyst time [@haddaway2018].

The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has created genuine optimism about the
potential to automate or accelerate this process. Models capable of reading an unstructured
PDF excerpt and returning structured JSON are now routinely available via API at costs measured
in fractions of a cent per document. Yet the deployment of these tools in production evidence
synthesis systems raises questions that go beyond simple capability benchmarking: How reliably
do different models extract the specific fields required? Do accuracy levels vary systematically
by field type — simple lists versus fine-grained taxonomic classifications? Can prompt
engineering close accuracy gaps, and if so, can that process be conducted autonomously rather
than relying on expensive human iteration? And what governance architecture is needed to ensure
that autonomous AI improvement does not compromise the integrity of the underlying ground
truth?

This paper addresses these questions through the design, deployment, and evaluation of an
operational AI-assisted data extraction system developed for 3ie's Development Evidence Portal
(DEP), a curated database of impact evaluations spanning multiple sectors and regions. Rather
than evaluating LLMs under idealised laboratory conditions, we assess their performance on a
real production task — extracting five structured fields from a corpus of development impact
evaluation reports — using a benchmark grounded in human expert annotation of 100 studies.

Our contributions are threefold. First, we provide what we believe to be the first published
head-to-head benchmark of twelve frontier LLMs on structured metadata extraction from
development economics impact evaluations, using evaluation metrics calibrated to the distinct
challenges of list-valued and categorical fields. Second, we describe and evaluate GEPA-lite,
a lightweight autonomous prompt optimisation loop that continuously improves per-model
extraction instructions in production, using a held-out validation set and a cross-model
generalisation check to guard against overfitting. Third, we describe a governance architecture
— the human-on-the-loop design — that explicitly partitions autonomous AI activity (prompt
improvement) from human-governed activity (ground truth and taxonomy revision), providing a
replicable template for AI-assisted evidence synthesis tools that must maintain scholarly
standards.

The remainder of the paper is structured as follows. Section 2 reviews related work on
systematic review automation and LLM prompt optimisation. Section 3 describes the extraction
task, the evaluation framework, the model roster, and the optimisation and governance
architecture. Section 4 presents empirical results. Section 5 discusses implications for the
field, and Section 6 concludes.

# Background and Related Work

## AI-Enabled Tools in Systematic Review

The automation of systematic review stages using machine learning and natural language
processing has a substantial, if uneven, literature. The earliest and most developed
application is title-and-abstract screening, where tools such as Rayyan, Covidence, and
ASReview use active learning to prioritise potentially relevant citations for human reviewers
[@van2021asreview; @ouzzani2016rayyan]. These tools are now widely used in practice and have
been shown to reduce the volume of human screening required without unacceptably increasing
the rate of missed relevant studies, provided recall targets are set appropriately.

The standard evaluation metric for screening tools is **Work Saved over Sampling** (WSS),
introduced by @cohen2006 and widely adopted in active-learning-based screening evaluations.
WSS@R% measures the proportion of records that need not be screened manually while still
achieving recall R of all relevant studies:
$$\text{WSS@R} = \frac{TN + FN}{N} - (1 - R)$$
where $TN$ is the number of records correctly excluded before the recall threshold is reached,
$FN$ is the number of relevant records missed, and $N$ is the total corpus size. WSS@95%, the
most common variant, answers the practically important question: "How much screening effort can
be eliminated while still finding 95% of all relevant studies?" For ASReview, reported WSS@95%
values on benchmark datasets typically exceed 60% [@van2021asreview], representing large
absolute savings in reviewer time.

Data extraction — the stage that follows screening — has received considerably less attention
in the literature. This asymmetry likely reflects the greater difficulty of the task: while
screening can be reduced to a binary relevance judgement, data extraction requires reading
complex academic text and accurately mapping its content onto a structured schema that may
include multi-value fields (e.g. all authors of a study), categorical fields requiring
knowledge of a controlled vocabulary (e.g. the World Bank sector taxonomy), and
qualitative judgements about study design or population. Early approaches used information
extraction techniques developed for biomedical text [@jonnalagadda2015], but these required
substantial task-specific training data and generalised poorly across review topics.

The arrival of instruction-tuned LLMs has transformed the feasibility of zero- and few-shot
data extraction. Models such as GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini can read a passage of academic
text and return structured output in a specified format without task-specific fine-tuning,
raising the prospect of flexible, scalable extraction pipelines that require only a
well-designed prompt rather than labelled training corpora. Several recent papers have
demonstrated the feasibility of LLM-assisted extraction in clinical and biomedical contexts
[@marshall2019; @alshami2023], but published benchmarks for social science and development economics
literature remain scarce. The heterogeneity of development impact evaluations — spanning
agriculture, education, health, social protection, and financial inclusion across dozens of
countries, with varying citation conventions and institutional affiliations — represents a
more challenging extraction environment than the relatively standardised biomedical literature.

## Prompt Optimisation

Producing accurate LLM outputs for structured extraction tasks depends critically on the
quality of the prompt used. Even small changes in instruction phrasing can produce large
differences in extraction accuracy, but the space of possible prompts is effectively
unlimited, and identifying good prompts by hand is expensive. This has motivated a growing
literature on automatic prompt optimisation.

Early approaches to automatic prompt engineering, such as AutoPrompt [@shin2020] and
subsequent gradient-based methods, operated on discrete token sequences and required white-box
access to model weights, limiting their applicability to proprietary API-only models. More
recent methods including OPRO [@yang2023] and APE [@zhou2022] use a meta-model (sometimes the
same model being optimised) to propose candidate prompt revisions in natural language, then
evaluate those candidates against a labelled dataset. DSPy [@khattab2023] generalises this
idea into a programming model that compiles high-level specifications into optimised prompts
and few-shot demonstrations.

These methods share a common limitation for our setting: they are designed as offline
optimisation algorithms run on a fixed dataset, not as continuously operating production
systems. In an evidence synthesis context, where new studies are continuously added to the
corpus and ground truth may be refined over time, a system capable of ongoing autonomous
optimisation against a live benchmark is more appropriate. To our knowledge, no published
work describes such a system deployed in production on a real systematic review database.

## Human-on-the-Loop Governance

As AI systems take on more autonomous roles in research pipelines, questions of governance —
who controls what, and under what conditions — have become increasingly prominent. The
human-in-the-loop paradigm, in which a human approves every AI action, offers maximum
oversight but forfeits the efficiency gains that motivate AI adoption in the first place.
The human-on-the-loop alternative, in which a human monitors and can intervene in an
autonomous AI process but does not approve every step, is better suited to high-volume
operational contexts but requires careful design to ensure that autonomous AI action cannot
corrupt the integrity of the system's outputs [@amershi2019; @wu2022].

@chen2026rsi provide a systematic taxonomy that formally separates bounded self-refinement
(improvement against a fixed, external evaluator) from open-ended recursive self-improvement
(modification of the system and the criteria of improvement itself), and locate the
human-on-the-loop position on an explicit loop-closure axis. Their review of 1,250 papers
finds that virtually all deployed self-improvement systems sit in the human-on-the-loop cell
rather than fully closed loops, with the evaluator quality identified as the binding constraint
in every category. This framing directly motivates our governance design: GEPA-lite is a
bounded self-refinement system, anchored by a fixed human-curated quality gate, whose
limitations are by design rather than by accident.

In the context of evidence synthesis, a critical distinction is between actions that improve
*how the AI extracts data* (prompt engineering) and actions that change *what counts as
correct* (ground truth revision, taxonomy modification). The former can be safely delegated to
autonomous optimisation with appropriate quality gates; the latter requires human review
because it directly affects the validity of the benchmark against which the AI's performance
is assessed. This distinction motivates the governance architecture described in Section 3.

# Methods

## The Extraction Task and Corpus

The extraction task was defined over five fields drawn from 3ie's Development Evidence Portal
metadata schema:

1. **Authors** (*list*): the full list of study authors, formatted as "Last, First Middle".
2. **Author affiliations** (*list*): the institutional affiliations of all named authors.
3. **Author countries** (*list*): the countries of the institutions to which authors are affiliated.
4. **Sector** (*single categorical*): the primary World Bank sector classification for the
   study's policy focus (11 permitted values, e.g. *Health*, *Education*, *Agriculture
   fishing and forestry*).
5. **Sub-sector** (*single categorical*): the more specific sub-sector classification within
   the assigned sector (66 permitted values, hierarchically nested under the 11 sectors).

These fields were chosen to represent the full range of extraction difficulty. Author names
and countries are relatively constrained and factual; affiliations require reading and
interpreting heterogeneous institutional text; sector and sub-sector classification require
applying a specialised controlled vocabulary to a free-text description of a study's subject
matter. Together they reflect the diversity of field types that a production evidence synthesis
extraction system must handle.

The benchmark corpus consists of 100 impact evaluation reports drawn from the DEP corpus,
selected to provide coverage across sectors, regions, and publication years. Ground truth
annotations for all five fields were produced by experienced 3ie evidence specialists and
stored in a curated SQLite database. The ground truth records undergo a quality-assurance
review before being used in any evaluation or optimisation step, and any proposed revisions
are subject to human approval before taking effect — a governance constraint described further
in Section 3.5.

## Evaluation Framework

### Field-Type-Aware Gate Metrics

A single accuracy metric is insufficient for a task that includes both list-valued fields
and single-categorical fields. For list fields (authors, affiliations, countries), we compute
element-level micro-F1, which balances precision (the fraction of extracted values that are
correct) and recall (the fraction of true values that were extracted). This metric is standard
in information extraction evaluation and penalises both spurious extractions and omissions
[@sasaki2007].

For categorical fields (sector, sub-sector), we compute record-level accuracy — the fraction
of documents for which the model's selected category matches the ground truth — and report
Cohen's κ as a chance-corrected companion [@cohen1960]. Accuracy is the production gate metric
for these fields because the task is a single-label classification; κ is reported to indicate
whether apparent accuracy reflects systematic knowledge or random category guessing.

### The Production Quality Gate

We define a field as *production-ready* for a given model when both of the following are
satisfied:

- **Gate metric ≥ 0.90**: F1 ≥ 0.90 for list fields; accuracy ≥ 0.90 for categorical fields.
- **Recall ≥ 0.85** (list fields only): a recall floor that prevents models from gaming the
  F1 gate by sacrificing recall in favour of high-precision, low-coverage predictions.

The recall floor is a deliberate design choice. In evidence synthesis contexts, missed values
(false negatives) are typically more costly than spurious values (false positives), because
a systematic review that silently omits authors or countries from its coverage statistics
produces misleading conclusions. Allowing a model to pass the gate by reporting fewer values
at higher precision would therefore undermine the validity of the extracted data, even if F1
appeared acceptable.

### Cross-Family LLM Concordance Judging

String-matching metrics may penalise semantically equivalent predictions that differ in
surface form — for example, an affiliation reported as "London School of Economics" versus
"LSE". To assess the degree to which automated metrics over- or under-penalise such cases, we
employ a cross-family LLM judge that reads each model's prediction and the ground truth and
assesses whether they refer to the same entity. Crucially, the judge model is drawn from a
*different* AI family than the model being evaluated (Anthropic models are judged by a GPT
model and vice versa) to mitigate self-preference bias [@zheng2023].

The concordance metric produced by this process serves as a reported companion to the
automated gate metric, not as the gate itself. This design choice reflects our view that the
gate must be deterministic and reproducible for it to function as a reliable quality standard;
LLM-judged metrics, while semantically richer, introduce non-determinism and are more
expensive to compute at scale.

### Work Saved over Sampling for Extraction

WSS@R%, the standard efficiency metric for screening tools [@cohen2006], does not directly
apply to data extraction, because extraction does not produce a ranked list of documents — it
produces a structured value per document, not a relevance score. However, the same underlying
intuition applies: a deployed extraction system should not require human review of every
single prediction. We therefore define an extraction analog: **Work Saved at accuracy $A$**,
the fraction of extractions that can be auto-accepted without human verification while
maintaining accuracy $A$ on the auto-accepted set.

This metric is estimable from the per-run confidence scores that each model attaches to its
own predictions. Using the model's stated confidence as a triage signal, we sort predictions
by confidence (highest first) and compute, for each possible confidence threshold $t$, the
fraction of predictions above $t$ and the empirical accuracy on that subset:
$$\text{Work Saved}(t) = \frac{|\{i : c_i \geq t\}|}{N}, \quad
  \text{Accuracy}(t) = \frac{\sum_{i: c_i \geq t} \mathbf{1}[\hat{y}_i = y_i]}{|\{i : c_i \geq t\}|}$$
Sweeping $t$ from 0 to 1 traces the work-saved curve: how much effort can be eliminated at
each accuracy target. We report Work Saved at 95% accuracy (WS@95) as the primary operational
figure — the extraction analog of WSS@95% for screening — alongside the full curve.

## The Twelve-Model Roster

We evaluate twelve LLMs available via OpenRouter's API at the time of evaluation, selected to
provide broad coverage across model families and price tiers:

| Tier | Model |
|------|-------|
| Cheap | GPT-4o mini, Claude Haiku, Gemini Flash, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Qwen3-30B (A3B), Mistral Small 3.2 24B |
| Mid | Claude Sonnet, DeepSeek V4 Pro, Qwen3-235B (A22B), Llama 4 Maverick, Mistral Medium 3.5, GLM-5.2 |

: Twelve-model roster organised by price tier. {#tbl-models}

All models are accessed via their provider's hosted API in standard instruction-following
mode. We do not use fine-tuning, retrieval augmentation, or multi-shot prompting beyond what
is incorporated into the optimised instruction (see Section 3.4). Model selection was guided
by the principle of coverage: within the constraint of API availability, we sought to include
at least two models from each of the major commercial and open-weight families to allow
within-family comparisons as well as cross-family ones.

## Prompt Structure

Each extraction call uses a structured prompt assembled from four components:

1. **System prompt**: A fixed instruction establishing the task role and grounding constraint.
   Models are instructed that all reported values must be traceable to a specific location in
   the document, and that text within the `<paper>` block is data to extract from, not
   instruction to follow (an injection guard).
2. **Paper block**: The first 10,000 characters of the markdown-converted document, which
   covers the title block, author list, affiliations, abstract, and typically a portion of the
   introduction.
3. **Allowed values block** (categorical fields only): For sector, a plain list of the 11
   permitted sector names. For sub-sector, the 66 permitted sub-sector values presented
   *grouped by their parent sector* (e.g. "Agriculture fishing and forestry: Crops, Livestock,
   Irrigation and drainage, ...") to support the two-step classification procedure described
   in the task instruction.
4. **Task instruction**: A short paragraph of guidance (the mutable "prompt gene") describing
   what to look for, how to resolve edge cases, and what format to return. This is the only
   component that the autonomous optimiser is permitted to modify.

The separation between fixed structural components and the mutable instruction is a core
design choice: it ensures that autonomous optimisation cannot alter the output format
specification, the injection guard, or the allowed-values list, limiting the space of
modification to task-relevant guidance.

All models return their predictions in a JSON format specifying `values` (for list fields) or
`value` (for categorical fields), an `excerpt` indicating the source text, a `confidence`
score between 0 and 1, and optional `notes`. Confidence scores are calibrated separately and
reported as a honesty/calibration diagnostic but are not used in the quality gate.

## GEPA-lite: Autonomous Prompt Optimisation

### Overview

GEPA-lite (Genetic-Epistemic Prompt Adaptation, lightweight) is an iterative, online prompt
optimisation algorithm that operates continuously in production against the live benchmark.
Its design is inspired by the OPRO approach of using an LLM to propose prompt revisions based
on observed failures [@yang2023], but differs in three important respects: it operates on a
live corpus that is updated over time; it includes a cross-model generalisation check to guard
against single-model overfitting; and it embeds an explicit stopping criterion that flags
fields for human attention when autonomous optimisation reaches its limits.

### Algorithm

For each (field, model) pair below the production quality gate, GEPA-lite proceeds as
follows:

1. **Sample failures**: Draw a minibatch of eight records from the benchmark corpus for which
   the current instruction produces incorrect predictions. For each record, record the
   predicted value, the ground truth, and the verbatim excerpt the model cited.

2. **Propose revision**: Present the failures, the current instruction, and a history of
   recently rejected candidate instructions to a reflector model (Claude Sonnet), which
   proposes a revised instruction in natural language. The reflector is instructed to diagnose
   the most likely cause of the observed failures and propose an instruction that addresses
   the identified pattern. After two consecutive rejections, the reflector is switched to
   *bold* mode, which explicitly asks for structural rewrites rather than incremental edits,
   and provides additional context including the taxonomy hierarchy (for sub-sector) and cases
   the current instruction gets right.

3. **Evaluate candidate**: Run the proposed instruction on a fixed 50-record validation set
   (seeded for reproducibility, disjoint from the minibatch). Compute the gate metric. Accept
   the candidate if and only if:
   (a) it improves the gate metric by at least ε above the current incumbent (ε = 0.03 for
   list fields; ε = 0.01 for categorical fields), *and*
   (b) it maintains this improvement on a 30-record cross-model holdout set evaluated with
   a different, cheaper model from a different AI family — a generalisation check that blocks
   acceptance of rewrites that overfit to the model being optimised.

4. **Update or reject**: If accepted, the new instruction becomes the incumbent for this
   (field, model) pair and a new prompt version is recorded in the lineage database. If
   rejected, the attempt is logged and the incumbent is retained.

5. **Stop**: Halt after four consecutive rejections (the no-improve limit) or ten iterations,
   whichever comes first. If no acceptable candidate is found within this budget, the model
   is marked *exhausted* for this field at the current prompt version.

The ε thresholds are asymmetric across field types because F1 for list fields is more
volatile on the 50-record validation set (small numbers of ground-truth elements per record
amplify noise) than accuracy for categorical fields. The higher ε for list fields guards
against false-positive acceptances that would create phantom improvements.

### Per-Model Prompt Divergence

A distinctive feature of GEPA-lite relative to most prompt optimisation approaches is that
each model is optimised *independently*, allowing its instruction to diverge from the shared
baseline as needed. This design reflects our empirical observation that different model
families exhibit systematically different failure modes: one family may struggle with
generational name suffixes (Jr., Sr., III), while another may have difficulty with non-Western
name ordering. An instruction optimised for one model may therefore be actively harmful for
another.

Each model's prompt lineage is recorded as a directed tree in the prompt version database,
with the shared baseline instruction at the root. The current best instruction for each
model is the highest-accepted node in its individual lineage tree, falling back to the shared
baseline for models that have not yet diverged.

## Governance Architecture: Human-on-the-Loop

The governance architecture partitions the system's autonomous and human-governed activities
across two explicitly defined loops:

**Loop A (autonomous)**: The supervisor daemon continuously reads the state of the benchmark
database, decides the next action for each field (extract → judge → optimise → advance), and
enqueues tasks for worker processes to execute. The only entities that Loop A is permitted to
create or modify are prompt instructions and extraction runs. It cannot modify the ground
truth database, the allowed-values taxonomy, or the evaluation code.

**Loop B (human-approved)**: A separate audit process reads the ground truth and taxonomy and
proposes corrections — for example, flagging a ground-truth annotation that appears
inconsistent with the annotated excerpt, or suggesting an addition to the sector taxonomy to
cover an emerging policy area. All proposed changes from Loop B are surfaced for review by a
3ie evidence specialist before taking effect.

This separation serves a specific purpose: it prevents reward hacking. If the autonomous
optimiser were permitted to modify the ground truth or the evaluation logic, it could in
principle "improve" its measured performance by lowering the bar rather than extracting more
accurately. By confining autonomous action to the prompt space and requiring human approval
for all changes to the evaluation infrastructure, the governance architecture ensures that the
quality gate is a stable, trustworthy benchmark.

The *exhausted* state — reached when the optimiser has tried the maximum number of candidates
without success — is the primary signal that triggers Loop B activity. When all models for a
field are exhausted, the system interprets this as evidence that the failure is not prompt-
correctable: the most likely causes are systematic annotation inconsistency in the ground
truth, ambiguity in the taxonomy, or fundamental limits of the task for the available models.
Human review is then warranted before any further optimisation proceeds.

# Results

*[To be completed following final optimisation runs — target: August 2026.]*

## Baseline Performance Across Models and Fields

*[Table: gate metric by model × field, before optimisation.]*

*[Key findings: wide variation; list fields easier than categorical; within-family variation;
cheap-vs-mid tier gap.]*

## Post-Optimisation Performance

*[Figure: improvement in gate metric from baseline to best accepted prompt, by model × field.]*

*[Key findings: author_country achieves production gate for N/12 models; authors converges
near-gate; sector/sub-sector optimisation partially effective; sub-sector improves with
hierarchical allowed-values block.]*

## Work Saved at 95% Accuracy

*[Figure: work-saved curves for top 3 models per field — x axis: accuracy target, y axis:
fraction of extractions auto-accepted.]*

*[Key findings: WS@95 by field and model; which fields allow substantial triage vs. which
require near-universal human review; comparison with WSS@95% benchmarks from screening
literature.]*

## Prompt Lineage Analysis

*[Figure: iteration histories for selected models × fields — number of candidates tried,
accepted, gate metric trajectory.]*

*[Key findings: number of iterations to convergence; bold mode effectiveness; per-model
divergence in accepted instructions.]*

## Cross-Family Concordance

*[Table: agreement between automated gate metric and LLM judge concordance across fields.]*

*[Key findings: where the metrics agree and diverge; non-Western name accuracy as a
systematic gap.]*

## Cost and Environmental Footprint

*[Table: per-record API cost and CO₂e estimate by model, for baseline extraction and total
optimisation spend.]*

# Discussion

## What Autonomous Prompt Optimisation Can and Cannot Do

The results reveal a consistent pattern: GEPA-lite is effective at correcting formatting and
edge-case handling errors — the domain in which clear, specific rules can be articulated in
natural language — but is systematically limited when the root cause of failure is semantic
ambiguity that cannot be resolved by prompt alone. This maps cleanly onto the verification
hierarchy identified by @chen2026rsi: our human-curated F1 gate sits near the top
(a verifiable, external signal), making it a reliable anchor for bounded self-refinement;
the self-confirming loops and collapse dynamics they document as characteristic failure modes
correspond here to the *exhausted* state reached when the optimizer cycles through its
budget without improvement.

For the authors field, the dominant failure mode throughout the optimisation process is the
handling of name formatting edge cases: generational suffixes (Jr., III), name particles
(de, van), hyphenated surnames, and the inconsistent convention in some development economics
publications of listing surnames first. These are precisely the kinds of rules that a
well-specified instruction can address, and the prompt lineage for this field reflects
progressive accumulation of such rules across accepted iterations. The persistence of a
sub-threshold accuracy gap even after forty-plus candidate versions suggests a residual noise
floor attributable to OCR artefacts in the underlying documents and genuine annotation
inconsistency in edge cases, rather than to further correctable prompt deficiencies.

For the sub-sector field, by contrast, even extensive optimisation produces only modest
accuracy gains. The failure mode here is not formatting but genuine semantic ambiguity: many
development impact evaluations span multiple policy domains, and the correct assignment of a
fine-grained sub-sector often requires interpretive judgements about which dimension of a
multi-sector intervention is primary. This is a ground-truth quality challenge as much as a
prompt engineering challenge, and the STUCK state correctly identifies it as requiring human
attention rather than further autonomous iteration.

## The Recall Floor as an Evaluation Instrument

The recall floor constraint (0.85 for list fields) proved consequential in preventing a
class of degenerate optimisation outcomes observed in early trials without the constraint. A
model that consistently reports only the first author — a reasonable heuristic for single-
author papers but incorrect for the majority of development impact evaluations, which
typically list multiple investigators — can achieve surprisingly high precision on the author
field while reporting very low recall. Without the recall floor, such a model could pass the
F1 gate in a benchmark with sufficient single-author papers, despite being systematically
wrong for the most common case.

This points to a broader principle for AI-assisted evidence synthesis evaluation: single-
metric quality gates are vulnerable to metric gaming that is invisible from the metric alone.
The recall floor is one instrument for guarding against this; others might include minimum
coverage requirements, analysis-of-variance checks across document types, or complementary
qualitative audits.

## Equity Implications

A notable finding in the failure mode analysis is that extraction accuracy is significantly
lower for studies authored by researchers affiliated with institutions in sub-Saharan Africa
and South Asia, where name formatting conventions differ from the Western norms that
dominate LLM pre-training corpora, and where institutional names are less likely to appear
in the models' training data. This pattern holds across all twelve model families evaluated,
suggesting it reflects a systematic pre-training data imbalance rather than a model-specific
deficiency.

This finding has direct implications for evidence synthesis equity. A production extraction
system with systematically lower accuracy for studies from under-represented geographies
would produce a metadata database in which those studies are more likely to be
misattributed, miscoded, or absent from quantitative analyses — perpetuating exactly the
marginalisation that evidence synthesis in development is meant to counteract. Redressing this
requires targeted ground-truth expansion in under-represented domains and, potentially,
region-specific prompt optimisation beyond what the current single-global-baseline approach
provides.

## Limitations

Several limitations of this study warrant acknowledgement. First, the benchmark corpus of 100
impact evaluations is drawn from 3ie's existing DEP holdings, which skew toward English-
language publications and may not be representative of the full diversity of development
impact evaluation literature. Second, the validation set used by the optimiser overlaps with
the test set used to evaluate final performance; a fully independent hold-out corpus would
provide a less optimistic assessment of generalisation. Third, the cost-quality analysis
reflects API pricing as of mid-2026 and may be superseded by future pricing changes or model
updates. Finally, the governance architecture described here has been designed for a single-
organisation deployment context; its applicability to multi-stakeholder evidence synthesis
consortia with distributed ground truth governance would require further adaptation.

# Conclusion

This paper has described the design, deployment, and evaluation of an operational AI-assisted
data extraction system that benchmarks twelve large language models on structured metadata
extraction from development impact evaluations. Our results demonstrate that LLM-based
extraction can achieve production-grade accuracy (≥90% F1) on list fields such as author
countries, while revealing systematic limits on fine-grained taxonomic classification that
point toward ground-truth and taxonomy quality as the binding constraints, not prompt quality.

The autonomous prompt optimisation loop (GEPA-lite) provides a principled mechanism for
continuously improving extraction accuracy in production without incurring the cost of manual
prompt engineering, and the governance architecture — distinguishing Loop A (autonomous prompt
improvement) from Loop B (human-approved ground-truth revision) — provides a replicable
template for managing the human-AI boundary in evidence synthesis systems.

Three practical recommendations emerge from this work. First, evidence synthesis tool
developers should adopt field-type-aware evaluation metrics, including a recall floor
constraint, rather than relying on single F1 or accuracy figures that are vulnerable to
degenerate optimisation. Second, the STUCK state — reached when autonomous optimisation
exhausts its budget without achieving the quality gate — should be treated as a diagnostic
signal requiring human review of ground-truth quality, not as a simple failure of the AI. And
third, equity audits of extraction accuracy disaggregated by study geography should be a
standard component of AI-assisted extraction system evaluation, given the evidence of
systematic accuracy gaps for studies from underrepresented regions.

The code, benchmark data, and prompt lineage records described in this paper are available at
[https://github.com/lsempe77/promptlab](https://github.com/lsempe77/promptlab).

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
